en.extract:
The great Indian bustard (Ardeotis nigriceps) or Indian bustard is a bustard occurring on the Indian subcontinent. It is a large bird with a horizontal body, long bare legs and is among the heaviest of the flying birds. Once common on the dry grasslands and shrubland in India, as few as 150 individuals were estimated to survive as of 2018, reduced from an estimated 250 individuals in 2011. It is critically endangered due to hunting and habitat loss. It is protected under the Indian Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972.


Description
The great Indian bustard is a large ground bird with a height of about one metre. It is unmistakable with its black cap contrasting with the pale head and neck. The body is brownish with a black patch spotted in white. The male is deep sandy buff coloured and during the breeding season has a black breast band. The crown of the head is black and crested and is puffed up by displaying males. In the female which is smaller than the male, the head and neck are not pure white and the breast band is either rudimentary, broken or absent.

Among bustards, this species is smaller only than the Kori bustard and the great bustard in size. It is also the largest land bird in its native range. The great Indian bustard stands at about 1 m (3 ft 3 in) tall, having a somewhat long neck and quite long legs. The female as in most members of the bustard family are typically considerably smaller.
Males have a well-developed gular pouch which is inflated when calling during display and helps produce the deep resonant calls.
Abnormally leucistic or near albino birds have been reported.


Distribution and habitat
The great Indian bustard was formerly widespread in India and Pakistan. In India, it historically occurred in Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. Today the bustard is restricted to isolated pockets in Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan (shared with Pakistan).
Today, the great Indian bustard occurs in Rajasthan, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat states of India. Desert National Park, near Jaisalmer and coastal grasslands of the Abdasa and Mandvi talukas of Kutch District of Gujarat support some populations. Ghatigaon and Karera sanctuaries in Madhya Pradesh once held sizeable populations. Other sanctuaries with the species include Kutch Bustard Sanctuary of Naliya in Kutch, Karera Wildlife Sanctuary in Shivpuri district; Great Indian Bustard Sanctuary near Nannaj,
It was also sighted near Solapur in Maharashtra, Shrigonda taluka in Ahmednagar district, near Nagpur and near Warora in Chandrapur district in Maharashtra and Rollapadu Wildlife Sanctuary, and near Kurnool in Andhra Pradesh. A few birds were detected in a September 2013 survey of the Cholistan Desert in Pakistan.
The great Indian bustard lives in arid, semi-arid grasslands, open country with thorn scrub, and tall grass interspersed with cultivation. It avoids irrigated areas. The major areas where it is known to breed are in central and western India and eastern Pakistan. The dry semi-desert regions of parts of Rajasthan have been transformed by irrigation canals into an intensively farmed area.


Behaviour and ecology
The great Indian bustard is omnivorous. It prefers insects, consisting mainly of Orthoptera, but also beetles including Mylabris species. It also consumes grass seeds, berries, largely of the genera Ziziphus and Eruca, rodents and reptiles; in Rajasthan, it also hunts Indian spiny-tailed lizards Uromastyx hardwickii. In cultivated areas, it feeds on crops such as exposed groundnut, millets and pods of legumes.
It drinks water if available and sometimes sits down to drink or suck water followed by raising up its heads at an angle. When threatened, hens are said to carry young chicks under the wing. Young birds have been recorded to dust-bathe frequently.
The male is polygamous. During the breeding season, it is solitary, but congregates to small flocks in winter. It is thought to use a mating system that has been termed as an "exploded or dispersed lek".
Great Indian bustards make local movements but these are not well understood; flocks disperse after the monsoon.
Breeding occurs between March and September, when the inflated fluffy white feathers of the male are inflated and displayed. Territorial fights between males may involve strutting next to each other, leaping against each other with legs against each other and landing down to lock the opponent's head under their neck. During courtship display, the male inflates the gular sac which opens under the tongue, inflating it so that a large wobbly bag appears to hang down from the neck. The tail is held cocked up over the body. The male also raises the tail and folds it on its back. The male periodically produces a resonant deep, booming call that may be heard for nearly 500m. The female lays a single egg in an unlined scrape on the ground. Only the females are involved in incubation and care of the young. The eggs are at risk of destruction from other animals particularly ungulates and crows. Females may use a distraction display that involves flying zigzag with dangling legs.


Threats
The great Indian bustard is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List since 2011. It has been extirpated in 90% of its former range, and the population was estimated at perhaps fewer than 250 individuals in 2008. The main threats are hunting and habitat loss. In the past they were heavily hunted for their meat and for sport and, today, poaching of the species may continue. In some places, such as Rajasthan, increased irrigation by the Indira Gandhi canal has led to increased agriculture and the altered habitat has led to the disappearance of the species from these regions. Current threats to the species include the development of linear infrastructure intrusions such as roads and electric power transmission lines in the desert that lead to collision-related mortality. Proposed expansion of renewable energy infrastructure, which may involve deploying solar panels over large areas of desert and grasslands is another threat to the bird's habitat. Some populations migrate into Pakistan where hunting pressure is high. The great Indian bustard is critically endangered in Pakistan primarily due to lack of protection and rampant hunting.
At Ranibennur Blackbuck Sanctuary, habitat changes have affected wildlife populations. In the 1950s, the scrub forest was replaced with Eucalyptus plantations. These helped wildlife when the trees were short but after their extensive growth they made the adjoining grassland less favourable for bustards.
Attempts to breed them in captivity in the 1970s failed.


Conservation
The state of Rajasthan initiated "Project Great Indian Bustard" on World Environment Day 2013, identifying and fencing off bustard breeding grounds in existing protected areas as well as provide secure breeding enclosures outside protected areas.
In 2020, nine chicks were incubated successfully creating a world record.
In 2024, a chick was hatched following artificial insemination of a female with sperm collected from a male 200 km away.
In March and April 2025, in the Jaiselmer district of Rajasthan, at the Sam Bustard conservation and breeding centre and the Ramdevra breeding centre, total of 8 chicks hatched out.


Evolution
The variability in mitochondrial DNA of the great Indian bustard showed low genetic diversity in 63 samples collected in five Indian states. This indicates a historical population reduction estimated to have happened about 20–40,000 years ago.


In culture
The Mughal emperor Babur noted that "[while] the flesh of the leg of some fowls, and of the breast of others is excellent; the flesh of every part of the Kharchal is delicious". The great Indian bustard was however a cryptic and wary bird making it a challenge for sportsmen, who had to stalk carefully (sometimes using covered bullock carts) to get within range. British soldiers in India considered it a delicacy and the species was among the top game-birds. William Henry Sykes notes that they were common in the Deccan region where a "gentleman" had shot a thousand birds. Stuart Baker however noted that this may have been an exaggeration. Jerdon noted that subadults and females had tastier flesh than males while Salim Ali notes that feeding on Mylabris (now Hycleus) tainted their flesh.
Tribal Bhils are claimed to have used a technique for trapping females that involves setting twigs on fire around the nest containing an egg or chick. The female was then said to run to the nest and singe its wings upon which the tribals captured it. Other trapping methods involving the use of nooses are described by Hume in his "Game Birds of India". The invention of the Jeep changed the method of hunting and it became extremely easy for hunters to chase bustards down in their open semi-desert habitats.
The name hoom is used in parts of Maharashtra and is derived from the low booming call. The sharp barking alarm call leads to its name of hookna in some parts of northern India. It is known in some other parts as Gaganbher or Gurayin for the resemblance of other calls to thunder or the roar of a tiger.
When the "national bird" of India was under consideration, the great Indian bustard was a proposed candidate (strongly supported by the Indian ornithologist Salim Ali), but dropped in favour of the Indian peafowl with at least one reason being the potential for being misspelt.


References


External links

Videos and photographs
Arkive - images and movies
BirdLife Species Factsheet

The '''great Indian bustard''' ('''''Ardeotis nigriceps''''') or '''Indian bustard''' is a [[bustard]] occurring on the [[Indian subcontinent]]. It is a large bird with a horizontal body, long bare legs and is among the heaviest of the flying birds. Once common on the dry grasslands and shrubland in India, as few as 150 individuals were estimated to survive as of 2018, reduced from an estimated 250 individuals in 2011. It is critically endangered due to hunting and [[habitat loss]]. It is protected under the Indian [[Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972]].

==Description==
The great Indian bustard is a large ground bird with a height of about one metre. It is unmistakable with its black cap contrasting with the pale head and neck. The body is brownish with a black patch spotted in white. The male is deep sandy buff coloured and during the breeding season has a black breast band. The crown of the head is black and crested and is puffed up by displaying males. In the female which is smaller than the male, the head and neck are not pure white and the breast band is either rudimentary, broken or absent.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen, P.C. |author2=Anderton, J.C. | year=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |volume=2 |page=148|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions}}</ref>

Among bustards, this species is smaller only than the [[Kori bustard]] and the [[great bustard]] in size. It is also the largest land bird in its native range. The great Indian bustard stands at about {{cvt|1|m}} tall, having a somewhat long neck and quite long legs.<ref name=hbk/> The female as in most members of the bustard family are typically considerably smaller.<ref name=fbi/>

Males have a well-developed [[gular pouch]] which is inflated when calling during display and helps produce the deep resonant calls.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|author1=Ali, S. |author2=Ripley, S.D. | pages=188–191|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan |publisher=Oxford University Press |edition=Second |place=Oxford |year=1980|isbn=0-19-565935-X}}</ref><ref name=hume>{{cite book |url=https://archive.org/stream/GameBirdsOfIndia1/HumeGameBirds1#page/n29/mode/1up|author1=Hume, A.O. |author2=Marshall, C.H.T. |pages=7–11|year=1879|title=Game birds of India, Burmah and Ceylon|volume=1}}</ref>

Abnormally [[leucism|leucistic]] or near albino birds have been reported.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Vijayarajji |year=1926 |title=An albino bustard (''Eupoditis edwarsi'')|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=31 |issue=2 |page=526 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47947217}}</ref>

<gallery widths="200" heights="200">
File:EupodotisEdwardsiBaker.jpg|Illustration by [[Henrik Grönvold]] from [[E. C. Stuart Baker]]'s ''Game-birds of India, Burma and Ceylon''
File:GIBustard_DSC0851.jpg|Male and female in display, Rajasthan
File:GreatIndianBustardHardwicke.jpg|From [[Thomas Hardwicke]]'s ''Illustrations of Indian Zoology'' (1830–1835)
File:BustardEggsFinn.jpg|Eggs of the species in comparison to the smaller ones of the [[lesser florican]]
</gallery>

==Distribution and habitat==
The great Indian bustard was formerly widespread in India and Pakistan.<ref name=fbi/> In India, it historically occurred in Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. Today the bustard is restricted to isolated pockets in Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan (shared with Pakistan).<ref name=DuttaRahmaniJhala2010>{{cite journal |author1=Dutta, S. |author2=Rahmani, A.R. |author3=Jhala, Y.V. |author3-link=Yadvendradev Vikramsinh Jhala |title=Running out of time? The great Indian bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' — status, viability, and conservation strategies| journal=European Journal of Wildlife Research| doi=10.1007/s10344-010-0472-z |date=2010 |url=http://www.conservationindia.org/wp-content/files_mf/Running-Out-of-Time.pdf| access-date=13 Jan 2014 |pages=615–625 |volume=57 |issue=3 |s2cid=31604255 |name-list-style=amp}}</ref>

Today, the great Indian bustard occurs in [[Rajasthan]], [[Karnataka]], [[Maharashtra]], [[Madhya Pradesh]] and [[Gujarat]] states of India. [[Desert National Park]], near [[Jaisalmer]] and coastal grasslands of the [[Abdasa Taluka|Abdasa]] and Mandvi talukas of [[Kutch District]] of Gujarat support some populations.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Munjpara, S. B. |author2=Jethva, B. |author3=Pandey, C.N. |title= Distribution of the Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Gruiformes: Otididae) in Gujarat State, India| journal=Journal of Threatened Taxa| year=2011| pages=2090–2094 |volume=3 |issue=9| doi=10.11609/jott.o2756.2090-4 |doi-access=free}}</ref> [[Ghatigaon Sanctuary|Ghatigaon]] and [[Karera]] sanctuaries in Madhya Pradesh once held sizeable populations.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Kasambe, R.|author2=Pimplapure, A. |author3=Thosar, G. |author4=Shad, M.S.R. |name-list-style=amp |year=2007 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/265652027 |title=Sighting records of Great Indian Bustards ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' in Vidarbha |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=46 |issue=6 |pages= 88–90}}</ref> Other sanctuaries with the species include [[Kutch Bustard Sanctuary]] of [[Naliya]] in Kutch,<ref name=DuttaRahmaniJhala2010/> [[Karera Wildlife Sanctuary]] in [[Shivpuri]] district; [[Great Indian Bustard Sanctuary]] near [[Nannaj]],<ref>{{cite journal|author=Kulkarni, B.S. |year=1981 |title=Ecology and behavior of Great Indian Bustard |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=78 |issue=2 |pages=375–377 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48228903}}</ref>
It was also sighted near [[Solapur]] in [[Maharashtra]], [[Shrigonda]] taluka in [[Ahmednagar]] district, near Nagpur and near Warora in Chandrapur district in Maharashtra and [[Rollapadu Wildlife Sanctuary]], and near [[Kurnool]] in [[Andhra Pradesh]].<ref name=rollapadu>{{cite journal |author1=Manakadan, R. |author2=Rahmani, A.R. |year=1989| title= Rollapadu Wildlife Sanctuary, with special reference to the Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=86 |issue=3 |pages=369–380 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710764}}</ref> A few birds were detected in a September 2013 survey of the [[Cholistan Desert]] in Pakistan.<ref>{{cite web|title=Houbara Foundation conducts survey for Great Indian Bustard |url=http://www.pakistantoday.com.pk/2013/09/17/city/lahore/houbara-foundation-conducts-survey-for-great-indian-bustard/ |work=Pakistan Today |date=2013| access-date=13 Jan 2014}}</ref>

The great Indian bustard lives in arid, semi-arid grasslands, open country with thorn scrub, and tall grass interspersed with cultivation. It avoids irrigated areas.<ref name=pcr/> The major areas where it is known to breed are in central and western India and eastern Pakistan. The dry semi-desert regions of parts of Rajasthan have been transformed by irrigation canals into an intensively farmed area.<ref name=pak>{{cite journal|title=Status, threats and conservation of the Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) in Pakistan |author=Khan, A.A. |author2=Khaliq, I. |author3=Choudhry, M.J.I. |author4=Farooq, A. |author5=Hussain, N. |name-list-style=amp |journal=Current Science |volume=95 |issue=8 |year=2008 |pages=1079–1082 |archive-date=2011-06-05 |url=http://www.ias.ac.in/currsci/oct252008/1079.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605032723/http://www.ias.ac.in/currsci/oct252008/1079.pdf}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
The great Indian bustard is [[omnivorous]]. It prefers [[insect]]s, consisting mainly of [[Orthoptera]], but also beetles including ''[[Mylabris]]'' species.<ref name="fbi" /> It also consumes [[grass seed]]s, [[berries]], largely of the genera ''[[Ziziphus]]'' and ''[[Eruca]]'', [[rodent]]s and [[reptile]]s; in Rajasthan, it also hunts Indian spiny-tailed lizards ''[[Uromastyx hardwickii]]''.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Gupta, P.D. |year=1975 |title=Stomach contents of the Great Indian Bustard ''Choriotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=71|issue=2| pages=303–304 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48286502}}</ref> In cultivated areas, it feeds on crops such as exposed groundnut, millets and pods of legumes.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Food and feeding behaviour of the Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) |author1=Bhushan, B. |author2=Rahmani, A.R. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year=1992|volume=89| issue=1 |pages=27–40|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48732480}}</ref>
It drinks water if available and sometimes sits down to drink or suck water followed by raising up its heads at an angle.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Wilson Bulletin |volume=106 |issue=4| year=1994 |pages=763–764 |title=Drinking methods in two species of bustards |author=Hallager, S.L. |hdl=10088/4338}}</ref> When threatened, hens are said to carry young chicks under the wing.<ref>{{cite journal |title=First Observations of Chick Carrying Behavior by the Buff-Crested Bustard |author=Falzone, C.K. |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=104 |issue=1| year=1992|pages=190–192 |jstor=4163135 }}</ref> Young birds have been recorded to dust-bathe frequently.<ref>{{cite journal |year=1963 |title=Rearing Great Indian Bustards (''Choriotis nigriceps'') |journal=Avicultural Magazine |issue=2 |pages=45–48 |volume=69 |author=Dharmakumarsinhji, K.S. |url=https://archive.org/details/aviculturalmaga691963asco/page/n74/mode/1up}}</ref>

The male is [[polygamous]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Rahmani, A.R. |year=1991 |title=Flocking behaviour of a resident population of the great Indian bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) |journal=Revue d'Écologie (La Terre et la Vie) |volume=46 |issue=1|pages=53–64 |doi=10.3406/revec.1991.2019 |hdl=2042/54637 |s2cid=191995652 |url=http://documents.irevues.inist.fr/bitstream/handle/2042/54637/RevuedEcologie_1991_46_1_53.pdf}}</ref> During the breeding season, it is solitary, but congregates to small flocks in winter.<ref>{{cite book |title=Game birds of India, Burma and Ceylon |volume=2|author=Stuart Baker, E.C. |year=1921 |publisher=Bombay Natural History Society |pages=164–185 |url=https://archive.org/stream/gamebirdsofindia02bake#page/n213/mode/2up}}</ref> It is thought to use a mating system that has been termed as an "exploded or dispersed [[Lek (mating arena)|lek]]".<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Ardeola| volume=48| issue=1| year=2001 |pages=85–98 |title=Exploded leks: What bustards can teach us |author1=Morales, M.B. |author2=Jiguet, F. |author3=Arroyo, B. |url=https://www.ardeola.org/uploads/articles/docs/474.pdf}}</ref>

Great Indian bustards make local movements but these are not well understood; flocks disperse after the [[monsoon]].<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Rahmani, A. R.  |author2=Manakadan, R.|title= Movement and flock composition of the Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) at Nanaj, Solapur district, Maharashtra, India |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year=1986 |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=17–31|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48760985}}</ref>

Breeding occurs between March and September, when the inflated fluffy white feathers of the male are inflated and displayed. Territorial fights between males may involve strutting next to each other, leaping against each other with legs against each other and landing down to lock the opponent's head under their neck.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Territorial fighting behaviour of Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' |author1=Joshua, J. |author2=Gokula, V. |author3=Sunderraj, S.F.W. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society | year=2005 |volume=102 |issue=1 |pages=114–115|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48378797}}</ref> During courtship display, the male inflates the gular sac which opens under the tongue, inflating it so that a large wobbly bag appears to hang down from the neck. The tail is held cocked up over the body. The male also raises the tail and folds it on its back. The male periodically produces a resonant deep, booming call that may be heard for nearly 500m.<ref name=pcr/><ref name=hbk/> The female lays a single egg in an unlined scrape on the ground.<ref name=fbi/><ref>{{cite book |pages=375–378 |title=The nests and eggs of the birds of India|author=Hume, A.O.|publisher=R. H. Porter |volume=3 |year=1890|url=https://archive.org/stream/nestseggsofindia03humerich#page/374/mode/1up}}</ref> Only the females are involved in incubation and care of the young. The eggs are at risk of destruction from other animals particularly ungulates and crows.<ref name=hbk/> Females may use a [[distraction display]] that involves flying zigzag with dangling legs.<ref name=hbk/>

== Threats ==
The great Indian bustard is listed as [[Critically Endangered]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] since 2011.<ref name=iucn/> It has been extirpated in 90% of its former range, and the population was estimated at perhaps fewer than 250 individuals in 2008.<ref name=iucn/> The main threats are hunting and habitat loss. In the past they were heavily hunted for their meat and for sport and, today, [[poaching]] of the species may continue. In some places, such as [[Rajasthan]], increased irrigation by the Indira Gandhi canal has led to increased agriculture and the altered habitat has led to the disappearance of the species from these regions.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Rahmani A.R. |author2=Soni, R.G. |year=1997| title=Avifaunal changes in the Indian Thar Desert|journal=Journal of Arid Environments |volume=36|pages=687–703 |doi=10.1006/jare.1996.0242|issue=4|bibcode=1997JArEn..36..687R }}</ref> Current threats to the species include the development of [[linear infrastructure intrusions]] such as [[roads]] and [[electric power transmission]] lines in the desert that lead to collision-related mortality.<ref>{{Cite report |title=Current ecological concerns in the power sector: options to avoid or minimise impacts |last1=Raman|first1=T. R.S. |last2=Madhusudan|first2=M.D.|date=2015 |url=http://ncf-india.org/publications/684|editor-last=Goswami|editor-first=M. N.|editor2-last=Chaudhry|editor2-first=P. |publisher=An Epochal Shift in the Idea of India-Meeting aspirations? IPPAI Knowledge Report, Independent Power Producers Association of India, New Delhi |pages=89–100}}</ref> Proposed expansion of renewable energy infrastructure, which may involve deploying solar panels over large areas of desert and grasslands is another threat to the bird's habitat.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://indianexpress.com/article/opinion/columns/rural-development-wildlife-protection-encroachment-great-indian-bustard-3023034/ |title=Bijli, sadak, paani and bustard|last=Dutta|first=Sutirtha|date=2016 |publisher=Indian Express|access-date=2016-09-10}}</ref> Some populations migrate into Pakistan where hunting pressure is high.<ref name=pak/> The great Indian bustard is critically endangered in Pakistan primarily due to lack of protection and rampant hunting.<ref name="Khanetal2008">{{cite journal|author=Khan, A.A. |author2=Khaliq, I. |author3=Choudhry, M.J. I. |author4=Farooq, A. |author5=Hussain, N. |name-list-style=amp|title=Status, threats and conservation of the Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps'' (Vigors) in Pakistan (1079)| journal=Current Science |volume=95 |issue=8 |date=2008 |pages=1079–1082 |url=http://www.currentscience.ac.in/Downloads/article_id_095_08_1079_1082_0.pdf}}</ref>

At [[Ranibennur Blackbuck Sanctuary]], habitat changes have affected wildlife populations. In the 1950s, the scrub forest was replaced with ''Eucalyptus'' plantations. These helped wildlife when the trees were short but after their extensive growth they made the adjoining grassland less favourable for bustards.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Neginhal, S.G.|year=1980| title=Ecological impact of afforestation at the Ranibennur Blackbuck Sanctuary| journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume =75| pages=1254–1258 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48297499}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Kumara, H.N. |author2=Raj, V.V.M. |year=2007|title=The Great Indian Bustard ''Ardeotis nigriceps''" Are they disappearing in Karnataka |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=104| issue=2|pages=211–212 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48380964}}</ref>

Attempts to breed them in captivity in the 1970s failed.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Sankhala, K. S. |year=1977| title=Captive breeding, reintroduction and nature protection: the Indian experience |journal=International Zoo Yearbook |volume=17|pages=98–101 |doi=10.1111/j.1748-1090.1977.tb00874.x}}</ref>

==Conservation==
The state of Rajasthan initiated "Project Great Indian Bustard" on [[World Environment Day]] 2013, identifying and fencing off bustard breeding grounds in existing protected areas as well as provide secure breeding enclosures outside protected areas.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rajasthan announces Project Great Indian Bustard |author=Shrivastava, K.S. |work=Down To Earth |date=2013 |url=http://www.downtoearth.org.in/content/rajasthan-announces-project-great-indian-bustard|access-date=7 June 2013}}</ref>
In 2020, nine chicks were incubated successfully creating a world record.<ref>{{cite news |title=India Succeeds in Breeding the 'Critically Endangered' Great Indian Bustard; Creates World Record |url=https://weather.com/en-IN/india/news/news/2020-01-02-india-succeeds-breeding-critically-endangered-great-indian-bustard |access-date=5 January 2020 |work=The Weather Channel |date=1 January 2020}}</ref>
In 2024, a chick was hatched following [[artificial insemination]] of a female with sperm collected from a male 200 km away.<ref>{{cite news |author1=Mollan, C. |title=Great Indian Bustard: Experts hail breakthrough in bid to save native bird |work=BBC |date=2024 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c62jv09249do |access-date=8 November 2024}}</ref>
In March and April 2025, in the [[Jaiselmer district]] of [[Rajasthan]], at the Sam Bustard conservation and breeding centre and the Ramdevra breeding centre, total of 8 chicks hatched out.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-05-16 |title=India-Pakistan Conflict Forces Relocation of 9 Endangered Great Indian Bustard Chicks |url=https://www.outlookbusiness.com/planet/climate/india-pakistan-conflict-relocation-great-indian-bustard-chicks |access-date=2025-05-18 |website=Outlook Business |language=en}}</ref>

== Evolution ==
The variability in mitochondrial DNA of the great Indian bustard showed low genetic diversity in 63 samples collected in five Indian states. This indicates a historical population reduction estimated to have happened about 20–40,000 years ago.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ishtiaq F. |author2=Dutta, S.  |author3=Yumnan, B.  |author4=Jhala, Y. |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/225627074 |title=Low genetic diversity in the endangered great Indian bustard (''Ardeotis nigriceps'') across India and implications for conservation |journal=Conservation Genetics |year=2011 |volume=12|issue=3 |pages=857–863 |doi=10.1007/s10592-011-0206-0|bibcode=2011ConG...12..857I |s2cid=43230093 }}</ref>

==In culture==
The Mughal emperor [[Babur]] noted that "[while] the flesh of the leg of some fowls, and of the breast of others is excellent; the flesh of every part of the ''Kharchal'' is delicious".<ref>{{cite journal|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |title=The Moghul emperors of India as naturalists and sportsmen. Part 2|author=Ali, S. |pages=34–63 |volume=32 |issue=1 |year=1927 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47859192}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|volume=2|title=The Babur-nama in English|author=Beveridge, A.S. |year=1922 |page=498 |url=https://archive.org/stream/baburnamainengli02babuuoft#page/498/mode/1up/}}</ref> The great Indian bustard was however a cryptic and wary bird making it a challenge for sportsmen, who had to stalk carefully (sometimes using covered bullock carts<ref>{{cite book |url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletshotinindi00russiala#page/380/mode/2up/|pages=381–382|title=Bullet and shot in Indian forest, plain and hill|author=Russell, C.E.M. |year=1900|publisher=W. Thacker and Co, London}}</ref>) to get within range.<ref>{{cite book|pages=33–34|title=Shikar sketches|author=Brown, J.M. |year=1887|publisher=Hurst and Blackett |url=https://archive.org/stream/shikarsketcheswi00browrich#page/32/mode/2up/search/eupodotis}}</ref> British soldiers in India considered it a delicacy and the species was among the top game-birds. [[William Henry Sykes]] notes that they were common in the Deccan region where a "gentleman" had shot a thousand birds.<ref>{{cite book|title=The Pictorial Family Encyclopaedia of History, Biography and Travels|author=Frost, J. |year=1854| publisher=Miller Orton and Mulligan|pages=95–96 |url=https://archive.org/stream/pictorialfamily00frosgoog#page/n103/mode/1up/search/nigriceps}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |url=https://archive.org/stream/proceedingsofzoo01zool#page/149/mode/1up|title=Catalogue of Birds of the Rasorial, Grallatorial and Natatorial Orders, observed in the Dukhun |author=Sykes, W.H. |year=1832|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |issue=Part 2 |pages=149–172}}</ref> Stuart Baker however noted that this may have been an exaggeration.<ref name=fbi/> Jerdon noted that subadults and females had tastier flesh than males while [[Salim Ali]] notes that feeding on ''Mylabris'' (now ''[[Hycleus]]'') tainted their flesh.<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite book|pages=607–611|title=The birds of India|author=Jerdon, T.C. |year=1864|publisher=George Wyman & Co. |volume=3 |url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsofindiabein03jerd#page/607/mode/1up/search/eupodotis}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |url=https://archive.org/stream/manualofgamebird01oaterich#page/398/mode/2up/ |pages=399–404|title=A manual of the game birds of India. Part 1.|author=Oates, E.W. |year=1898 |publisher=A. J. Combridge & Co}}</ref>

Tribal [[Bhils]] are claimed to have used a technique for trapping females that involves setting twigs on fire around the nest containing an egg or chick. The female was then said to run to the nest and singe its wings upon which the tribals captured it.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Simcox, A.H.A. |year=1913| title= The Great Indian Bustard ''Eupodotis edwardsi''| journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=22|issue=1|page=201|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30155114}}</ref> Other trapping methods involving the use of nooses are described by [[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]] in his "Game Birds of India".<ref name=hume/> The invention of the Jeep changed the method of hunting and it became extremely easy for hunters to chase bustards down in their open semi-desert habitats.<ref name=hbk/>

The name ''hoom'' is used in parts of Maharashtra and is derived from the low booming call. The sharp barking alarm call leads to its name of ''hookna'' in some parts of northern India.<ref name=fbi>{{cite book|author=Baker, E.C.S.|year=1929|title=The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma |volume=6|edition=Second|pages=64–66 |url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds6/BakerFBI6#page/n99/mode/1up}}</ref> It is known in some other parts as ''Gaganbher'' or ''Gurayin'' for the resemblance of other calls to thunder or the roar of a tiger.<ref>{{cite journal|author= Elliot, W.|author-link=Walter Elliot (ICS)|year=1880| title=Notes on the Indian Bustard (''Eupodotis edwardsi''), with especial reference to its gular pouch|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |pages=486–489|url=https://archive.org/stream/proceedingsofgen80zool#page/486/mode/2up}}</ref>

When the "national bird" of India was under consideration, the great Indian bustard was a proposed candidate (strongly supported by the Indian ornithologist [[Salim Ali (ornithologist)|Salim Ali]]<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW1#page/n14/mode/1up|volume=1|issue=4|year=1961|author=Ali, S. |title=Our national bird|pages=3–4 |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |volume=1|issue=6|page=4|title=National bird|author=Ali, S. |year=1962 |url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW1#page/n23/mode/1up |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] }}</ref>), but dropped in favour of the [[Indian peafowl]] with at least one reason being the potential for being misspelt.<ref>{{cite journal |title=On the brink|journal=Tehelka Magazine|author=Bindra, P.S. |year=2009|volume=6|issue=16 |url=http://www.tehelka.com/story_main41.asp?filename=hub250409extinction.asp |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100131120617/http://www.tehelka.com/story_main41.asp?filename=hub250409extinction.asp |archive-date=2010-01-31}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title= The Peacock Cult in Asia|author= Nair, P. T. |journal= Asian Folklore Studies|volume= 33|issue= 2|year= 1974|pages= 93–170|url= http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/afs/pdf/a272.pdf |doi= 10.2307/1177550|jstor= 1177550|access-date= 2010-02-20|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20090205195010/http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/afs/pdf/a272.pdf|archive-date= 2009-02-05}}</ref>

zh.extract:
南亞鴇（學名：Ardeotis nigriceps），又名大印度鴇或黑冠鷺鴇，是分布於印度及巴基斯坦東部的鴇。牠們受到失去棲息地的威脅。


特徵
南亞鴇是陸禽，頸部及腳都很長，身體呈褐色及白色。牠們站立時高約1米，雄鳥長1.22米及重8-14.5公斤，雌鳥長92厘米及重3.5-6.75公斤。雄鳥及雌鳥外觀上相似，但雄鳥的毛色呈較深的沙黃色。頭頂黑色，有冠。雌鳥較雄鳥細小，頭及頸都不是純白色，未必有胸部斑紋。


行為
南亞鴇於3月至9月繁殖，雄鳥於此時會脹大及向雌鳥示愛。雄鳥會豎起尾巴到背上，摺曲頸部，並發出很深及很急的叫聲。
南亞鴇是一夫多妻制的。雌鳥每年只會生一蛋，約需27天來孵化，雄鳥並不會參與任何孵化或餵養雛鳥的工作。牠們會在開揚的地上築巢，故鳥蛋經常受到其他動物的破壞。雛鳥會與母鳥一同生活，直至下一個繁殖季節到來。雄鳥於繁殖季節是獨居的，而其他時間則會組成群落。


分佈棲息地
南亞鴇棲息在乾旱至半乾旱的草原，長滿高草及針刺叢林，但會避開灌溉地區。牠們分佈在印度的拉賈斯坦邦、卡納塔克邦、馬哈拉施特拉邦及中央邦。


食性
南亞鴇是雜食性的，主要吃甲蟲、草蜢、種子及地上的堅果。


保育狀況
南亞鴇的數量估計少於1000隻。其主要威脅是因灌溉而失去棲息地。一些群落遷徙至巴基斯坦後受到捕獵的影響。牠們已經消失於中央邦的卡若拉（Karera）保護區。


參考

"Ecology and Behavior of Bustard" B.N.H.S Journal Vol 78(2),28-8-81,375-77
